From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1954 science fiction giant monster film by Gordon Douglas

For other uses, see [Them (disambiguation)](/wiki/Them_(disambiguation) "Them (disambiguation)").

| Them! | |
| --- | --- |
| Theatrical release poster | |
| Directed by | [Gordon Douglas](/wiki/Gordon_Douglas_(director) "Gordon Douglas (director)") |
| Screenplay by | [Ted Sherdeman](/wiki/Ted_Sherdeman "Ted Sherdeman") [Russell Hughes](/wiki/Russell_S._Hughes "Russell S. Hughes") (adaptation) |
| Story by | [George Worthing Yates](/wiki/George_Worthing_Yates "George Worthing Yates") |
| Produced by | [David Weisbart](/wiki/David_Weisbart "David Weisbart") |
| Starring | [James Whitmore](/wiki/James_Whitmore "James Whitmore") [Edmund Gwenn](/wiki/Edmund_Gwenn "Edmund Gwenn") [Joan Weldon](/wiki/Joan_Weldon "Joan Weldon") [James Arness](/wiki/James_Arness "James Arness") |
| Cinematography | [Sidney Hickox](/wiki/Sidney_Hickox "Sidney Hickox") |
| Edited by | Thomas Reilly |
| Music by | [Bronislau Kaper](/wiki/Bronislau_Kaper "Bronislau Kaper") |
| Production company | [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") |
| Distributed by | Warner Bros. |
| Release dates | * June 16, 1954 (1954-06-16) (New York)[[1]](#cite_note-1) * June 18, 1954 (1954-06-18) (United States) |
| Running time | 94 minutes |
| Country | United States |
| Language | English |
| Box office | $2.2 million (rentals)[[2]](#cite_note-Variety-2) |

***Them!*** is a 1954 [black-and-white](/wiki/Black-and-white "Black-and-white") [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") [giant monster film](/wiki/Giant_monster_film "Giant monster film") starring [James Whitmore](/wiki/James_Whitmore "James Whitmore"), [Edmund Gwenn](/wiki/Edmund_Gwenn "Edmund Gwenn"), [Joan Weldon](/wiki/Joan_Weldon "Joan Weldon"), and [James Arness](/wiki/James_Arness "James Arness").[[3]](#cite_note-Them!-3) Produced by [David Weisbart](/wiki/David_Weisbart "David Weisbart"), the film was directed by [Gordon Douglas](/wiki/Gordon_Douglas_(director) "Gordon Douglas (director)"), based on an original story by [George Worthing Yates](/wiki/George_Worthing_Yates "George Worthing Yates") that was developed into a [screenplay](/wiki/Screenplay "Screenplay") by [Ted Sherdeman](/wiki/Ted_Sherdeman "Ted Sherdeman"), with adaptation by [Russell Hughes](/wiki/Russell_S._Hughes "Russell S. Hughes").

The film is one of the first of the 1950s "nuclear monster" movies, and the first with [big bugs](/wiki/Arthropods_in_film "Arthropods in film")
as the threat. A nest of [gigantic irradiated](/wiki/Enlargement_(in_fiction)#Excessive_growth "Enlargement (in fiction)") [ants](/wiki/Ant "Ant") is discovered in the New Mexico desert, and they quickly become a national threat when it is found that two young queens and their consorts have escaped to establish new colonies. The national search that follows culminates in a battle with the last surviving colony in the concrete [spillways](/wiki/Spillways "Spillways") and storm drain system of Los Angeles.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1954, [New Mexico State Police](/wiki/New_Mexico_State_Police "New Mexico State Police") Sergeant Ben Peterson and his partner, Ed Blackburn, discover a little girl wandering the desert in a catatonic state near [Alamogordo](/wiki/Alamogordo "Alamogordo"), and the ravaged and abandoned travel trailer she came from a few miles away. The officers summon an ambulance for the girl, and, while the scene is processed, go to a nearby rural general store. They find it destroyed in the same way as the trailer, and the owner's dead, mutilated body in the cellar. No money was taken at either crime scene. Peterson leaves Blackburn alone to wait for backup, and Blackburn hears a strange, pulsating sound outside. He investigates, and empties his gun at something before screaming in agony.

The owner of the trailer is found to be an FBI agent, so Agent Robert Graham is called in from the local FBI office to help Peterson. He sends a cast of a print found near the trailer to Washington, D.C., for analysis, and is surprised when he receives word that Drs. Harold and Pat Medford, father and daughter [myrmecologists](/wiki/Myrmecology "Myrmecology"), are being sent by the [Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture"). On an educated hunch, Harold exposes the little girl to the scent of [formic acid](/wiki/Formic_acid "Formic acid"), which is characteristic of ants. The odor releases her from her catatonia, leaving her shrieking in panic, "Them! Them!"

Peterson and Graham take the Medfords to where the trailer was found. While searching the area, Pat is accosted by a giant foraging ant, and Harold directs Peterson and Graham to shoot off the ant's antennae to blind it, before Peterson kills it with a [Thompson submachine gun](/wiki/Thompson_submachine_gun "Thompson submachine gun"). Harold theorizes that the ants were mutated by radiation from [the first atomic bomb test](/wiki/Trinity_(nuclear_test) "Trinity (nuclear test)") near Alamogordo, and have turned carnivorous from lack of a suitable food supply in the desert.

A helicopter search of the desert discovers a giant ant nest, surrounded by human remains. [Cyanide](/wiki/Cyanide "Cyanide") gas bombs are tossed inside, and Graham, Peterson, and Pat enter to explore. They are attacked by a pair of surviving guard ants, which they dispatch with [flamethrower](/wiki/Flamethrower "Flamethrower") and Thompson. At the lowest level, Pat finds evidence that two queens have hatched and flown away. Harold declares that, if the queens are allowed to establish new colonies and the ants continue to spread, mankind may cease to be Earth's dominant species within a year.

The search area is widened, though the reason for the operation is kept top secret to avoid mass panic. Graham and Pat travel to [Brownsville, Texas](/wiki/Brownsville,_Texas "Brownsville, Texas"), to interrogate a ranch foreman committed to a mental hospital after claiming he was forced down in his plane by [UFOs](/wiki/Unidentified_flying_object "Unidentified flying object") shaped like giant ants. One ant queen is discovered to have boarded a freighter in [Acapulco](/wiki/Acapulco "Acapulco"), Mexico, when, out in the Pacific Ocean, its offspring emerge and attack the crew. The [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") [cruiser](/wiki/Cruiser "Cruiser") that responds to the distress call finds only two surviving sailors in the water, and the freighter is sunk by naval gunfire.

A report about a shipment of stolen sugar leads the investigators to a Los Angeles hospital, where a chronic [alcoholic](/wiki/Alcoholism "Alcoholism") claims he has seen giant ants in the city's storm drain system. When they also trace the movements of a man whose mutilated body was found in the area back to the drains and discover another giant ant print, they are convinced that both the final missing queen and the man's two missing young sons are somewhere in the over 700 miles of tunnels under the city.

Martial law is declared in Los Angeles, and a curfew imposed. Peterson, Graham, and the Medfords help the armed forces search the drain network, and Peterson finds the boys trapped by some ants. He calls for reinforcements and is just able to lift the boys to safety before being attacked and killed. Graham arrives with backup, and the queen and her hatchlings are located. The Medfords confirm that no new queens have gotten away and sanction the destruction of the nest. Graham wonders what havoc might ensue from all of the other nuclear explosions in the past decade, and Harold responds that mankind has entered a new world, and no one can predict what we will find.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [James Whitmore](/wiki/James_Whitmore "James Whitmore") as Sergeant Ben Peterson of the [NMSP](/wiki/New_Mexico_State_Police "New Mexico State Police")
* [Edmund Gwenn](/wiki/Edmund_Gwenn "Edmund Gwenn") as Dr. Harold Medford of the [USDA](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture"), Pat's father
* [Joan Weldon](/wiki/Joan_Weldon "Joan Weldon") as Dr. Pat Medford of the USDA, Harold's daughter
* [James Arness](/wiki/James_Arness "James Arness") as [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") Agent Robert Graham
* [Onslow Stevens](/wiki/Onslow_Stevens "Onslow Stevens") as General O'Brien of [Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") [Intelligence](/wiki/Military_intelligence "Military intelligence")
* [Sean McClory](/wiki/Sean_McClory "Sean McClory") as Major Kibbee of Air Force Intelligence
* [Chris Drake](/wiki/Chris_Drake "Chris Drake") as Trooper Ed Blackburn of the NMSP
* [Sandy Descher](/wiki/Sandy_Descher "Sandy Descher") as the Ellinson girl, who is found wandering alone in the New Mexico desert
* Mary Alan Hokanson (credited Mary Ann Hokanson) as Mrs. Lodge, whose two young sons are missing in Los Angeles
* Don Shelton as Captain Fred Edwards of the NMSP
* [Fess Parker](/wiki/Fess_Parker "Fess Parker") as Alan Crotty, foreman of the Lazy K Ranch in [Brownsville, Texas](/wiki/Brownsville,_Texas "Brownsville, Texas")
* [Olin Howland](/wiki/Olin_Howland "Olin Howland") (credited Olin Howlin) as Jensen, an alcoholic patient who has seen strange things in Los Angeles

Uncredited

* [William Schallert](/wiki/William_Schallert "William Schallert") as the ambulance attendant in New Mexico
* Joseph Forte as "Doc" Putnam, a county medical officer in New Mexico
* [Ann Doran](/wiki/Ann_Doran "Ann Doran") as the child psychiatrist
* [Willis Bouchey](/wiki/Willis_Bouchey "Willis Bouchey") as an official at the meetings in Washington, D.C.
* [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy") as the Air Force Sergeant delivering paperwork
* [Dick Wessel](/wiki/Dick_Wessel "Dick Wessel") as the railroad detective in Los Angeles
* [Dub Taylor](/wiki/Dub_Taylor "Dub Taylor") as the railroad yard watchman in Los Angeles
* [John Beradino](/wiki/John_Beradino "John Beradino") as Patrolman Ryan of the [LAPD](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department")
* [Lawrence Dobkin](/wiki/Lawrence_Dobkin "Lawrence Dobkin") as the Los Angeles city engineer

### Cast notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cast notes")]

* [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy") has a small, uncredited part as a U.S. Army staff sergeant in the communications room.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4) Other actors who appear in small, uncredited parts include [Booth Colman](/wiki/Booth_Colman "Booth Colman"), [Walter Coy](/wiki/Walter_Coy "Walter Coy"), [Richard Deacon](/wiki/Richard_Deacon_(actor) "Richard Deacon (actor)"), [Dorothy Green](/wiki/Dorothy_Green_(actress_born_1920) "Dorothy Green (actress born 1920)"), [Jan Merlin](/wiki/Jan_Merlin "Jan Merlin"), Douglas Spencer, Harry Tyler, and [Harry Wilson](/wiki/Harry_Wilson_(actor) "Harry Wilson (actor)").
* When casting his planned [Davy Crockett](/wiki/Davy_Crockett_(TV_miniseries) "Davy Crockett (TV miniseries)") episode of the *[Disneyland](/wiki/Walt_Disney_anthology_television_series "Walt Disney anthology television series")* television series, [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") viewed the film to see James Arness, who had been recommended for the title role. Disney, however, was more impressed by a scene with Fess Parker as an inmate in the mental ward of the Texas hospital. Watching Parker's performance, Disney realized he had found his Davy Crockett.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2012167-5) [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne") saw the film and, impressed with Arness' performance, recommended him for the role of [Marshal Matt Dillon](/wiki/Matt_Dillon_(Gunsmoke) "Matt Dillon (Gunsmoke)") in the new *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")* TV series, a role that Arness would play from 1955 to 1975.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4d/Themtitle.jpg/250px-Themtitle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Themtitle.jpg)

Opening title card with the background in [black and white](/wiki/Black_and_white "Black and white") and "Them!" in red and blue

When *Them!* began production in the fall of 1953, it was originally conceived to be in [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film") and Warnercolor. During pre-production, test shots in color and 3D were made. A few color tests of the large-scale ant models were made, but, when it was time to shoot the 3D test, Warner Bros.' "All Media" 3D camera rig malfunctioned, and no footage could be filmed. The next day, a memo was sent out that the color and 3D aspects of the production were to be scrapped. While widescreen black-and-white would now be the film's presentation format, because of the preparation of certain scenes, many of the camera set-ups designed for 3D remain in the film, such as the opening titles and the flamethrower shots aimed directly at the camera.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerzberg2007176-6)

Although Warner Bros. was dissatisfied with the color results, the film's title was printed in vivid red and blue against a black-and-white background to give the film's opening a dramatic "punch". This effect was achieved by an [Eastmancolor](/wiki/Eastmancolor "Eastmancolor") section spliced into each release print.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4) The 1985 release of the film on [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") tape, as well as the later [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc"), [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") releases, have retained this black-and-white-with-two-color title effect.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The scenes set in the [Chihuahuan Desert](/wiki/Chihuahuan_Desert "Chihuahuan Desert") of southern New Mexico were actually filmed in the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") near [Palmdale, California](/wiki/Palmdale,_California "Palmdale, California"). Mercy Hospital was a real institution, and is now Brownsville Medical Center. The entrance to the ants' final nest was filmed along the concrete spillways of the [Los Angeles River](/wiki/Los_Angeles_River "Los Angeles River"), between the First and Seventh Street Bridges, east of downtown.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4)

The giant ants, painted a purplish-green color, were constructed and operated by unseen technicians supervised by Ralph Ayers. During the climactic battle sequence in the Los Angeles storm drains, there is a brief shot of one ant moving in the foreground with its side removed, revealing its mechanical interior,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4) though this filming error was obscured on the DVD and Blu-ray releases of the film.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [stridulation](/wiki/Stridulation "Stridulation") sounds produced by the giant ants in the film were created by mixing recordings of the calls of [bird-voiced tree frogs](/wiki/Bird-voiced_tree_frog "Bird-voiced tree frog") together with recordings of the calls of a [wood thrush](/wiki/Wood_thrush "Wood thrush"), [hooded warbler](/wiki/Hooded_warbler "Hooded warbler"), and [red-bellied woodpecker](/wiki/Red-bellied_woodpecker "Red-bellied woodpecker"). These were recorded at Indian Island, Georgia, on April 11, 1947, by the [Cornell Lab of Ornithology](/wiki/Cornell_Lab_of_Ornithology "Cornell Lab of Ornithology").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[James Whitmore](/wiki/James_Whitmore "James Whitmore") wore "lifts" in his shoes to compensate for the height difference between himself and James Arness. Whitmore also employed bits of "business" (hand gestures and motions) during scenes in which he appeared, in order to draw more attention to his character when not speaking.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4)

The [Wilhelm scream](/wiki/Wilhelm_scream "Wilhelm scream"), created three years earlier for the film *[Distant Drums](/wiki/Distant_Drums "Distant Drums")*, is used during several of the film's action sequences. It can be heard when a sailor aboard the freighter is grabbed by an ant, when Whitmore's character is caught in an ant's [mandibles](/wiki/Mandible_(insect_mouthpart) "Mandible (insect mouthpart)"), and when an overhead wooden beam falls on a soldier in the Los Angeles storm drain sequence.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4)

The film's poster shows a gigantic ant with menacing cat-like eyes, rather than the normal [compound eyes](/wiki/Compound_eye "Compound eye") of an ant.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Box office")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Tri-City_Drive-In_Ad_-_22_June_1954%2C_Loma_Linda%2C_CA.jpg/250px-Tri-City_Drive-In_Ad_-_22_June_1954%2C_Loma_Linda%2C_CA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tri-City_Drive-In_Ad_-_22_June_1954,_Loma_Linda,_CA.jpg)

[Drive-in](/wiki/Drive-in_theater "Drive-in theater") advertisement from 1954

*Them!* was released in June 1954,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4) and by the end of that year had accrued $2.2 million (US) in [distributors](/wiki/Film_distributor "Film distributor")' domestic (U.S. and Canada) rentals, making it the year's 51st biggest earner.[[2]](#cite_note-Variety-2)[[8]](#cite_note-Gebert-8)[[Note 1]](#cite_note-9)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Critical response")]

[A. H. Weiler](/wiki/A._H._Weiler "A. H. Weiler")'s review of the film in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* noted that "from the moment James Whitmore, playing a New Mexico state trooper, discovers a six-year-old moppet wandering around the desert in a state of shock, to the time when the cause of that mental trauma is traced and destroyed, *Them!* is taut science fiction".[[9]](#cite_note-NYTrvw-10)

The reviewer for *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* called the film a "top-notch science fiction shocker. It has a well-plotted story, expertly directed and acted in a matter-of-fact style to rate a chiller payoff and thoroughly satisfy the fans of hackle-raising melodrama".[[10]](#cite_note-Brog-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)

[John McCarten](/wiki/John_McCarten "John McCarten") of *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* wrote: "If you're willing to let your imagination off its leash, you may have a fairly good time at *Them!*"[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The contemporary *[Monthly Film Bulletin](/wiki/Monthly_Film_Bulletin "Monthly Film Bulletin")* stated that, despite the science fiction film genre being new, it had developed several sub-divisions, including "the other-worldly, the primeval-monstrous, the neo-monstrous, the planetary-visitant, etc.", and that *Them!* is a "well-built example of the neo-monstrous", "less absurdly sensational than most".[[13]](#cite_note-mfb-14) It called the film's ant monsters "reasonably horrible--they do not entirely avoid the impression of mock-up that is almost inevitable when over-lifesize creatures have to be constructed and moved", and said they are "considerably more conceivable than those prehistoric remnants that have recently been emerging from bog and iceberg".[[13]](#cite_note-mfb-14) The review also noted that, "like most science-fiction, [the film] is on the whole serviceably rather than excitingly cast", described the direction as "smoothly machined", and said the film has "decent writing", though it claimed that "more short cuts might have been [taken]", as the start of the film was too slow.[[13]](#cite_note-mfb-14)

Since its original release, *Them!* has become generally regarded as one of the best science fiction films of the 1950s. [Bill Warren](/wiki/Bill_Warren_(film_historian_and_critic) "Bill Warren (film historian and critic)") described it as "tight, fast-paced and credible ... the picture is suspenseful".[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1982188–195-4) The entry on the film in [Phil Hardy](/wiki/Phil_Hardy_(journalist) "Phil Hardy (journalist)")'s *[The Aurum Film Encyclopedia: Science Fiction](/wiki/The_Aurum_Film_Encyclopedia:_Science_Fiction "The Aurum Film Encyclopedia: Science Fiction")* states: "Directed by [Gordon] Douglas in semi-documentary fashion, *Them!* is one of the best American science fiction films of the fifties".[[14]](#cite_note-Hardy-15) [Danny Peary](/wiki/Danny_Peary "Danny Peary") said the film "Ranks with *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_from_Another_World "The Thing from Another World")* and *[Invasion of the Body Snatchers](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Body_Snatchers "Invasion of the Body Snatchers")* as the best of the countless '50s science fiction films".[[15]](#cite_note-Peary-16) In the *[Time Out Film Guide](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")*, [David Pirie](/wiki/David_Pirie "David Pirie") wrote: "By far the best of the 50s cycle of 'creature features' ... retains a good part of its power today".[[16]](#cite_note-Pirie-17)

In 2023, film scholar Foster Hirsch proposed that the picture is a subtle anti-communist allegory, pointing out that: "the ants are red, they work underground, their colony is well organized, and their numbers mushroom with alarming speed . . . the ants are pointed directly at the city where the film industry is located, an industry at the time accused with being overrun with Reds." As further evidence, he points to a scene where police are searching a ruined general store while a radio commentator in the background discusses "an unstable political situation" and asks if the "Cold War has heated up."[[17]](#cite_note-18)

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), 93% of 57 critics' reviews of the film are positive, with an average rating of 7.7/10; the site's "critics consensus" reads: "One of the best creature features of the early atomic age, *Them!* features effectively menacing special effects and avoids the self-parody that would taint later monster movies".[[18]](#cite_note-RottenTom-19)

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Awards")]

At the [27th Academy Awards](/wiki/27th_Academy_Awards "27th Academy Awards"), *Them!* was nominated for [Best Special Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects"),[[19]](#cite_note-oscars-20) though it lost to *[20,000 Leagues Under the Sea](/wiki/20,000_Leagues_Under_the_Sea_(1954_film) "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea (1954 film)")*. It won a [Golden Reel Award](/wiki/Golden_Reel_Award_(Motion_Picture_Sound_Editors) "Golden Reel Award (Motion Picture Sound Editors)") for Best Sound Editing.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Them!&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* *Them!* heavily inspired the 1956 Japanese *[kaiju](/wiki/Kaiju "Kaiju")* film *[Rodan](/wiki/Rodan_(film) "Rodan (film)")*.[[20]](#cite_note-21)
* [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison")'s band [Them](/wiki/Them_(band) "Them (band)") was named after this film,[[21]](#cite_note-22) though the band member that came up with the name was not Morrison, but [Eric Wrixon](/wiki/Eric_Wrixon "Eric Wrixon").[[22]](#cite_note-23)
* The [Amiga](/wiki/Amiga "Amiga") video game series *[It Came from the Desert](/wiki/It_Came_from_the_Desert "It Came from the Desert")* was inspired by *Them!*[[23]](#cite_note-CGW-24)
* *[Fallout 3](/wiki/Fallout_3 "Fallout 3")*, which takes place in a post-apocalyptic irradiated wasteland, has a side-quest involving giant mutated fire ants titled "Those!" in homage to the film.[[24]](#cite_note-25)
* The scene where Pat is attacked by the foraging ant appears in the 2018 [Marvel film](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films") *[Ant-Man and the Wasp](/wiki/Ant-Man_and_the_Wasp "Ant-Man and the Wasp")*, when Scott, Hope, and Cassie watch *Them!*[[25]](#cite_note-26)
* The scenes from *Them!* appears in the 2002 [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") film *[Lilo & Stitch](/wiki/Lilo_%26_Stitch "Lilo & Stitch")* and its 2005 [direct-to-video sequel](/wiki/Lilo_%26_Stitch_2:_Stitch_Has_a_Glitch "Lilo & Stitch 2: Stitch Has a Glitch").
